Curriculum

Week 2 — Language and Regulations

June 29 – July 3 · NLP tokens & embeddings · HIPAA deep dive · First extraction task

W2

Language and Regulations

Jun 29 – Jul 3  ·  NLP + HIPAA/GDPR/GLBA  ·  Deliverable: error analysis of 2 wrong predictions

Learning Goals

  • Understand what tokens, embeddings, and transformers are at an intuitive level
  • Know the core structure of HIPAA and why it's hard for AI
  • Run a batch extraction experiment and inspect the raw JSON outputs
  • Write your first substantive error analysis

Day 1 — Monday, June 29: How LLMs Read Text

🧠 Topics

Tokenization

  • LLMs process tokens, not words: "anti-inflammatory" = 3 tokens
  • Token limits: Gemma3:4b has an 8k context window
  • Why this matters: longer scenarios may get truncated
  • Interactive demo: run the tokenizer on a HIPAA scenario

Embeddings & Attention

  • Embeddings: words as points in high-dimensional space
  • Similar words are geometrically close
  • Attention: "he signed the order" — court order or coffee order?
  • This is why transformers handle legal context well (usually)

Live Coding: Inspect LLM Outputs

We'll open a raw JSON extraction from ComplianceGPT and examine what the LLM actually output for a HIPAA scenario. We'll look at the fields: sender_role, receiver_role, purpose, phi_involved. What did the model get right? What did it miss or make up?

🎯 Day 1 Task

Run batch_runner.py on 20 rows from data/goldcoin_hhs_merged.csv. Save results to results/[yourname]_week2_batch.csv. Open it in a spreadsheet. Find 1 wrong prediction and write 2 sentences about why you think it's wrong.

Day 2 — Wednesday, July 1: HIPAA Deep Dive

⚖️ Topics

HIPAA Structure

  • Who must comply: covered entities (CEs) and business associates
  • What is PHI: 18 identifiers under the Privacy Rule
  • The general rule: no disclosure without authorization
  • The exceptions: ~15 permitted disclosure categories

Key Permitted Disclosures

  • Treatment, payment, healthcare operations (TPO) — most common
  • Patient authorization — explicit written consent
  • Law enforcement — with specific legal instruments
  • Judicial proceedings — court orders, subpoenas
  • Research — with IRB waiver or de-identification
  • Public health — disease reporting, FDA, CDC

The Minimum Necessary Rule

Even permitted disclosures must use only the minimum necessary PHI for the stated purpose. A hospital can share a diagnosis for treatment, but not an entire medical history. ComplianceGPT checks this as a sub-condition on many disclosure categories.

🎯 Day 2 Task

Read the HIPAA Summary document (link on readings page). For 3 wrong predictions from your batch, categorize the HIPAA exception that applies: TPO, law enforcement, judicial, research, or public health. Write 1 sentence per scenario explaining which exception is relevant and whether the model got the category right.

Day 3 — Friday, July 3: GDPR and GLBA Overview

🌍 Topics

GDPR

  • Covers all personal data of EU residents (not just health)
  • Lawful basis: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest
  • Data subject rights: access, erasure, portability, objection
  • Maximum fine: 4% global revenue or €20M

GLBA

  • US financial privacy law (1999)
  • Banks must protect customer NPI (non-public personal info)
  • Customers have opt-out rights for sharing with third parties
  • Analogy: HIPAA for finance — same structure, different domain

Discussion: Transferability

ComplianceGPT was built for HIPAA and extended to GDPR, GLBA, and SOX. Your project might study: does a model that's good at HIPAA also transfer well to GDPR? Or do different regulations require different prompting strategies? We'll explore this question together.

Week 2 Deliverable

Due: Friday, July 4 (Independence Day — submit by end of July 3)

1. Python exercise set — Complete exercises 5–8 in notebooks/week2_nlp.ipynb: token counting, batch accuracy computation, precision/recall calculation, results filtering.

2. Error analysis (1 page) — Pick 2 wrong predictions from your batch run. For each:

  • What was the scenario? (1 sentence summary)
  • What did the model predict? What was the correct answer?
  • Looking at the raw JSON extraction — what field was wrong? (sender_role? purpose? phi_involved?)
  • What would you change about the prompt or the model to fix it?

Recommended Reading This Week

  • HIPAA Privacy Rule Summary (hhs.gov) — The 2-page summary is enough. The full regulation is 100+ pages; you don't need it all.
  • Vaswani et al. — "Attention Is All You Need" (2017) — Section 1 (introduction) and Section 3 (architecture diagram) only. This is the foundational transformer paper.
  • Andrej Karpathy — "Let's Build GPT from Scratch" (YouTube) — Optional but strongly recommended. You will genuinely understand transformers after this.
